items.title: Shaken baby syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Shaken baby syndrome

  • Pathophysiology: violent acceleration-deceleration of the skull on a lax neck, produces tearing of bridging veins and subdural hematoma; Cerebral edema is the most common cause of death.
  • Classic triad: retinal hemorrhage (bilateral, multilayered), subdural hematoma (usually posterior interhemispheric), and encephalopathy (irritability, lethargy, seizures).
  • Key finding: the absence of a skull fracture does not rule out diagnosis; diffusion-weighted MRI shows diffuse axonal injury.
  • Differential diagnosis: accident due to fall from height <1.5 m rarely produces retinal hemorrhage; rule out coagulation disorders and inborn errors of metabolism.

items.title: Glove/sock immersion burns
items.contentHtml:
Burns due to immersion in a glove/sock

  • Definition: thermal burn that affects hands or feet with a clear horizontal limit ("in a glove" or "in a sock"), secondary to forced immersion in hot water.
  • Pathognomonic pattern: respects palmar/plantar folds (the child flexes fingers when immersed) and has a linear upper border and well demarcated, without splashes.
  • Differential diagnosis: accidental scald burn usually has irregular edges, splashes and asymmetric distribution; immersion produces symmetry and uniform level.
  • Management: classify depth (second or third degree) and refer to burn unit; Suspected abuse requires immediate legal notification.

items.foco: Fractures in different stages of consolidation
items.title: Fractures in different stages of consolidation
items.contentHtml:
Fractures in different stages of consolidation

  • Indicator of abuse: the simultaneous presence of acute, healing and consolidated fractures in the same patient suggests repeated episodes of non-accidental trauma.
  • Radiological stages: periostitis appears at 7-10 days, soft callus at 2-3 weeks, hard callus at 4-8 weeks and remodeling months later.
  • Minimal study: complete skeleton x-ray (bone series) in children <2 years with suspicion; repeat after 2 weeks to detect occult fractures in consolidation.
  • Differential diagnosis: osteogenesis imperfecta (blue sclerae, dentinogenesis, family history) and rickets (metaphyseal widening, costal rosary).

items.foco: Negligence as the most frequent form
items.title: Negligence as the most frequent form
items.contentHtml:
Neglect as the most frequent form

  • Definition: omission of basic care (feeding, hygiene, supervision, medical care) that puts the health or development of the child at risk; constitutes >50% of cases of abuse.
  • Clinical indicators: malnutrition, stunted weight, rampant cavities, severe diaper rash, recurrent untreated infections, lack of vaccination.
  • Indicators of medical negligence: non-compliance with chronic treatments (asthma, diabetes), failure to attend health check-ups, delay in seeking care for illnesses acute.
  • Management: evaluate risk of imminent harm; if serious, hospitalize and notify child protective services; Treatment includes family support and multidisciplinary follow-up.

items.foco: Legal obligation to report by health personnel
items.title: Legal obligation to report
items.contentHtml:
Legal obligation to report by healthcare personnel

  • Legal basis: in most countries, healthcare professionals have legal obligation to report any reasonable suspicion of child abuse to the competent authority (police, prosecutor's office or protection service).
  • Characteristics: the report is mandatory and immediate; does not require diagnostic certainty, only well-founded suspicion; Failure to comply constitutes a crime of failure to report.
  • Confidentiality: the duty to report prevails over professional secrecy; no parental authorization is required.
  • Documentation: record objective findings in the medical history (description of injuries, photographs with metric scale, sketches) and the communication made; This legally protects the professional.

items.foco: Retinal detachment as a sign of abuse
items.title: Retinal detachment
items.contentHtml:
Retinal detachment as a sign of abuse

  • Association: Tractional or exudative retinal detachment, together with multilaminar retinal hemorrhages, is highly specific for shaken baby syndrome.
  • Pathophysiology: acceleration-deceleration causes retinal tear by vitreous traction; subhyaloid hemorrhage can progress to detachment.
  • Diagnosis: indirect ophthalmoscopy under mydriasis; ocular ultrasound confirms detachment when there is media opacity.
  • Differential diagnosis: severe accidental trauma (car accident, fall from a height >3 m), retinopathy of prematurity, retinoblastoma (tumor mass).
  • Trap: the absence of retinal hemorrhage does not rule out abuse; detachment may be the only ocular manifestation.

items.title: Broken labial frenulum
items.contentHtml:
Break of the labial frenulum in infants

  • Indicator: the break of the upper or lower labial frenulum in an infant is highly suggestive of abuse due to forced introduction of an object (bottle, spoon, penis) into the oral cavity.
  • Mechanism: direct trauma against the frenulum; accidental falls rarely injure it due to its protected position.
  • Differential diagnosis: congenital short frenulum (there is no break in continuity or hematoma), Aphtha ulcers (non-traumatic aphthous lesions).
  • Management: careful inspection of the oral cavity; look for other injuries (palatal ecchymosis, broken teeth); surgical repair is rarely necessary; legal notification must be made.

items.title: Grip Pattern Bruises
items.contentHtml:
Bruises in hand-gripping pattern

  • Description: parallel linear ecchymoses or two separate rounded bruises (fingerprints) on the arms, thighs, chest or cheeks, corresponding to the pressure of the aggressor's fingers.
  • Typical location: lateral side of the arm (thumb) and medial side (remaining fingers) when holding the child to shaking or immobilizing it.
  • Differential diagnosis: purpura due to vasculitis (does not follow a finger pattern), friction dermatitis (erythema without hematoma), bandage marks (regular linear shape).
  • Evaluation: measure and photograph with scale; bruises in children who are not crawling or ambulating are suspicious; The presence of multiple bruises in different stages of color reinforces the suspicion.

items.foco: Transfer late or inconsistent with the injury
items.title: Late or inconsistent transfer
items.contentHtml:
Transfer late or inconsistent with injury

  • Indicator of abuse: Delay in seeking medical attention for a serious injury (fracture, burn, head trauma) or explanation that does not correlate with severity or mechanism are classic red flags.
  • Typical inconsistencies: caregiver reports a fall from bed (<1 m) but the child has a subdural hematoma or complex skull fracture; bilateral injury that the caregiver attributes to a unilateral event.
  • Evaluation: interview the caregiver without confrontation, document the history verbatim, contrast with the biomechanics of the injury; inconsistency is a criterion to activate the abuse protocol.
  • Management: hospitalize for multidisciplinary evaluation and protection; Do not register until the security of the environment is clarified.

items.title: Spiral femur fracture
items.contentHtml:
Spiral fracture of the femur in non-ambulators

  • Association: the spiral fracture of the femur in a child who does not walk (under 12-18 months) is highly suspicious of abuse, since it requires a twisting mechanism (rotational force applied to the limb).
  • Alternative accidental mechanism: fall from a height or car accident; in non-ambulators, a fall from the bed or changing table rarely produces a spiral femoral fracture.
  • Differential diagnosis: osteogenesis imperfecta (multiple fractures, blue sclerae), fibrous dysplasia, pathological fracture due to bone tumor.
  • Management: radiography of the affected limb and complete bone series; if abuse is confirmed, immobilization and legal notification; fracture in children <1 year old has a positive predictive value >90% for abuse.

items.foco: Syphilis or gonorrhea in prepubertal children as an indicator
items.title: Sexually transmitted infections in prepubertal children
items.contentHtml:
Syphilis or gonorrhea in prepubertal children as an indicator

  • Indicator of sexual abuse: the presence of syphilis (Treponema pallidum) or gonorrhea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae) in a prepubertal child (under 12 years of age) is practically pathognomonic of sexual abuse, except for vertical transmission perinatal.
  • Diagnosis: culture or nucleic acid amplification test in urethral, vaginal, pharyngeal or rectal secretions; serology for syphilis (VDRL/RPR confirmed with FTA-ABS).
  • Differential diagnosis: non-sexual contact infection (fomites) is extremely rare; vertical transmission of syphilis manifests itself in the first 2 years of life; rule it out due to maternal history.
  • Management: treat with antibiotics according to guidelines (ceftriaxone for gonorrhea, penicillin G for syphilis); perform mandatory legal notification and evaluation by sexual abuse team.

items.foco: Comprehensive protection law for children and adolescents
items.title: Comprehensive protection law
items.contentHtml:
Law for the comprehensive protection of children and adolescents

  • Guiding principle: the best interests of the child is the primary criterion in any decision that affects them; The law establishes the right to physical, psychological and sexual integrity.
  • Obligations of health personnel: detect, report and document cases of abuse; Failure to report is punishable administratively and criminally.
  • Protection measures: the authority may order the separation of the aggressor, the hospitalization of the child, the suspension of visitation or the loss of parental authority.
  • Procedure: the complaint is made before the family court or the public ministry; The health team must preserve the child's privacy and avoid revictimization during the interview and examination.
